commentary: Six activities this week versus thirteen last week, which is a significant drop in volume, and honestly the body probably needed it — last week you were doubling up almost every day like someone who had something to prove, and by Wednesday you were pushing a 2000m swim at 149 bpm which is not a chill way to close out a big week. This week looks more like a human being made thoughtful choices: the Mosman circuit came in at 13.3km against last week's 13.5km, essentially the same run, but your heart rate was 155 bpm compared to 149 bpm, which suggests you went a little harder or your legs were carrying some accumulated fatigue from everything that preceded it. The Prince Alfred kick set on Thursday at 143 bpm was a solid effort, and Tuesday's return to the pool at 130 bpm over 1500m reads like someone who was genuinely recovering rather than just calling something a recovery and then absolutely sending it, which — unlike that 150 bpm "recovery" run last Tuesday — is the correct interpretation of the word. The pattern here is someone who ran very hot for a big week and then intelligently pulled back, and that discipline is its own kind of gorgeous.
metrics_commentary: Okay, 4250 metres in the pool is not a casual splash around — that is a proper, committed, beautiful piece of work, and you should feel every bit of it. The 13.3 kilometres of run/walk tells me you got outside and moved your body through the world, which counts, though one session means there's more on the table next week if you want it. Your HRV is the real story here: a latest read of 62 against a seven-day average of 51 means your body is recovering well and trending upward, which is your nervous system basically telling you it's having a good time. And a VO2 Max of 56.7 is quietly elite-adjacent — that engine is genuinely impressive, and it deserves to be taken out more than once a week.
gym_commentary: Two sessions out of four, babe — that's a half-week and we both know it, so we're not going to pretend otherwise. Volume dropped from 24,518 kg down to 20,148 kg, which tracks exactly with showing up half as much, so there's nothing mysterious happening here, just fewer appearances. No new PRs either, though a 168 kg leg press and a 107.9 kg trap bar deadlift are not numbers a man apologises for carrying around — those are the numbers of someone whose body is doing serious work when he actually walks through the door. Four sessions is the target, two is the gap, and Thursday's 73-minute workout at least tells me that when you're there, you're there — now let's see all of you there next week.
